title: Crossing the 12,000-atom barrier with heterogeneous quantum-classical supercomputing: quantum chemistry of protein-ligand complexes
authors: Kenneth M. Merz,, Akhil Shajan, Danil Kaliakin, Fangchun Liang, Yuichi Otsuka, Tomonori Shirakawa, Lukas Broers, Han Xu, Miwako Tsuji, Mitsuhisa Sato, Seiji Yunoki, Ryo Wakizaka, Yukio Kawashima, Jun Doi, Toshinari Itoko, Hiroshi Horii, Thaddeus Pellegrini, Javier Robledo Moreno, Kevin J. Sung, Ella Fejer, Robert Walkup, Seetharami Seelam, Mario Motta
abstract: Ab initio wavefunction methods provide accurate molecular simulations but their computational scaling restricts applications to small systems. We develop a workflow combining quantum embedding to decompose a molecule into fragments with a heterogeneous quantum-classical (HQC) method to simulate fragments. We sample fragment electronic configurations on two 156-qubit quantum processors (ibm$\_$cleveland, ibm$\_$kobe), using up to 94 qubits, running 9,200 circuits for over 100 hours, collecting $1.3 \cdot 10^9$ measurement outcomes - the most resource-intensive HQC computation for quantum chemistry to date. We compute fragment wavefunctions via optimized subspace diagonalization on two supercomputers (Fugaku, Miyabi-G), achieving 72.5$\%$ parallel efficiency with scalable distributed linear algebra kernels. We simulate two protein-ligand complexes spanning dispersion- and electrostatics-dominated regimes (11,608 and 12,635 atoms), demonstrate $>40\times$ increase in system size and up to $210\times$ improvement in accuracy over the previous state-of-the-art, with HQC matching coupled-cluster (CCSD) accuracy in fragment energies, and establish a scalable pathway for systematically improvable biomolecular simulations.
summary: This paper presents a breakthrough in computational chemistry by using a hybrid quantum-classical supercomputing workflow to simulate protein-ligand complexes containing over 12,000 atoms. By combining quantum processors for fragment sampling with GPU-accelerated supercomputers for diagonalization, the researchers achieved unprecedented accuracy and system size. This work establishes a scalable pathway for using quantum technologies to study complex biomolecular systems.
main_problem: The computational scaling of accurate ab initio wavefunction methods limits their application to small molecules, creating a gap between accessible system sizes and the large protein-ligand complexes relevant to drug discovery.
main_result: The authors successfully crossed the 12,000-atom barrier using a heterogeneous quantum-classical workflow, achieving a 40x increase in system size and a 210x improvement in accuracy over previous state-of-the-art methods.
method: A heterogeneous quantum-classical (HQC) approach using quantum embedding (EWF) to decompose molecules into fragments, combined with a new TrimSQD fragment solver and GPU-accelerated subspace diagonalization (SBD-G).
model_or_system: Large protein-ligand complexes, specifically Trypsin (12,635 atoms) and T4-Lysozyme (11,608 atoms), modeled using the time-independent many-electron Schrodinger equation and IBM quantum processors (ibm_cleveland, ibm_kobe) alongside Fugaku and Miyabi-G supercomputers.
key_observables: Fragment energies, binding energies, and the 2-particle reduced density matrix (2-RDM).
important_parameters: System sizes exceeding 12,000 atoms; use of up to 94 qubits; 1.3e9 measurement outcomes; spatial truncation radii (R_cut = 7A, R_buf = 3A); and parallel efficiency of 72.5%.
main_assumptions: The use of a minimal STO-3G basis set and the assumption of local entanglement to justify the fragment-based embedding approach.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the protein systems and the HQC algorithmic scheme; Figure 2 compares ExtSQD and TrimSQD workflows; Figure 5 shows energy difference improvements; Figure 6 presents the accuracy-time tradeoff.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the scientific challenge, details the HQC workflow and new algorithmic innovations (EWF, TrimSQD, SBD-G), describes the hardware platforms (Quantum and HPC), presents results on large protein-ligand complexes, and discusses scaling and future directions.
why_interesting: It demonstrates a large-scale implementation of quantum-classical hybrid algorithms, showing how quantum sampling can be integrated into high-performance computing workflows to solve many-body electronic structure problems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Systems investigated (A), overarching algorithmic scheme adopted, (B), representation of a fragment (C), details of the lower-scaling fragment construction (D) introduced in this work.

Fig. 2. The workflow of ExtSQD (left) and TrimSQD (middle), with central innovations from this study marked in light yellow (right). “Cfg.” is an abbreviation for “electronic configurations”, x in the main text.

Fig. 3. Strong scaling of TrimSQD with baseline threshold ε = 5 · 10−6. In each point, the median of parallel efficiency is annotated (left and center). Elapsed time breakdown in the atom 178 (right).
Fig. 4. Strong scaling of matrix–vector multiplication (Nsub = 232).
Fig. 5. Accuracy improvement of TrimSQD over ExtSQD.
